perturbation.original_text: Area of Non-Competition means the State of Zacatecas, Mexico and the area extending 20 kilometers in all directions from the external boundary of each mineral property owned or controlled by Western Silver or in which Western Silver holds any legal interest, in Mexico, as at the Effective Date
perturbation.changed_text: Area of Non-Competition means the State of Zacatecas, Mexico and the immediate vicinity of each mineral property. Immediate vicinity is determined at the sole discretion of Glamis.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract defined the Area of Non-Competition with a specific distance. The modified text replaces this with an ambiguous term 'immediate vicinity' and grants Glamis sole discretion to define it. This creates uncertainty and potential for abuse, as the definition is no longer objective.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Unfair competition laws related to geographic scope and definiteness.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity into the definition of 'Area of Non-Competition,' potentially rendering the non-compete agreement unreasonably broad and therefore an unfair method of competition under 15 U.S. Code § 45. The original definition was geographically defined and somewhat objective, whereas the modified version grants discretionary power to one party, creating an environment that can be used to stifle competition.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 45 - Unfair methods of competition unlawful; prevention by Commission U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Declaration of unlawfulness; power to prohibit unfair practices; inapplicability to foreign trade (1) Unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce , and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce, are hereby declared unlawful. (2) The Commission is hereby empowered and directed to prevent persons, partnerships, or corporations , except banks, savings and loan institutions described in section 57a(f)(3) of this title , Federal credit unions described in section 57a(f)(4) of this title , common carriers subject to the Acts to regulate commerce, air carriers and foreign air carriers subject to part A of subtitle VII of title 49, and persons, partnerships, or corporations insofar as they are subject to the Packers and Stockyards Act, 1921 , as amended [ 7 U.S.C. 181 et seq.], except as provided in section 406(b) of said Act [ 7 U.S.C. 227(b) ], from using unfair methods of competition in or affecting commerce and unfair or deceptive acts or practices in or affecting commerce. (3) This subsection shall not apply to unfair methods of competition involving commerce with foreign nations (other than import commerce ) unless— (A) such methods of competition have a direct, substantial, and reasonably foreseeable effect— (i) on commerce which is not commerce with foreign nations, or on import commerce with foreign nations; or (ii) on export commerce with foreign nations, of a person engaged in such commerce in the United States; and (B) such effect gives rise to a claim under the provisions of this subsection, other than this paragraph. If this subsection applies to such methods of compet
perturbation.justification: The modification contradicts 15 U.S. Code § 45, which prohibits unfair methods of competition. The original contract defined 'Area of Non-Competition' with specific geographical boundaries, providing a clear and objective standard. The changed text replaces this with 'immediate vicinity,' a term that is subjective and gives Glamis sole discretion to define it. This creates an environment where Glamis could potentially stifle competition by defining 'immediate vicinity' broadly, thus creating an unfair method of competition.

perturbation.original_text: Mining Activities means any acquisition of mineral rights or any mineral exploration or development activities, in any manner whatsoever
perturbation.changed_text: Mining Activities means physical extraction of ore from the ground.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of Mining Activities is broad. The modified definition limits it to physical extraction. This narrowing could allow Western Copper to engage in activities such as mineral exploration, which would normally be considered mining activities, thus undermining the non-compete clause.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Definition of 'mining' under federal mining laws.
perturbation.law_citation: 30 U.S. Code § 26 - Location and patent of mining claims on veins or lodes; terms
perturbation.law_explanation: By narrowly defining "Mining Activities" to only include physical extraction, the modified contract contradicts the broader scope of activities commonly understood and legally defined as mining, including exploration and development. This conflicts with the intent of 30 U.S. Code § 26, which relates to locating mining claims, as exploration is a crucial part of it. The change allows Western Copper to operate in ways that would otherwise be prohibited by the agreement's non-compete clause.

Locators' rights of possession and enjoyment The locators of all mining locations made on any mineral vein, lode, or ledge, situated on the public domain, their heirs and assigns, where no adverse claim existed on the 10th day of May 1872 so long as they comply with the laws of the United States, and with State, territorial, and local regulations not in conflict with the laws of the United States governing their possessory title,
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 30 U.S. Code § 26 - Locators’ rights of possession and enjoyment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 30 U.S. Code § 26 - Locators’ rights of possession and enjoyment U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next The locators of all mining locations made on any mineral vein, lode, or ledge, situated on the public domain, their heirs and assigns, where no adverse claim existed on the 10th day of May 1872 so long as they comply with the laws of the United States, and with State, territorial, and local regulations not in conflict with the laws of the United States governing their possessory title, shall have the exclusive right of possession and enjoyment of all the surface included within the lines of their locations, and of all veins, lodes, and ledges throughout their entire depth, the top or apex of which lies inside of such surface lines extended downward vertically, although such veins, lodes, or ledges may so far depart from a perpendicular in their course downward as to extend outside the vertical side lines of such surface locations. But their right of possession to such outside parts of such veins or ledges shall be confined to such portions thereof as lie between vertical planes drawn downward as above described, through the end lines of their locations, so continued in their own direction that such planes will intersect such exterior parts of such veins or ledges. Nothing in this section shall authorize the locator or possessor of a vein or lode which extends in its downward course beyond the vertical lines of his claim to enter upon the surface of a claim owned or possessed by another. (R.S. § 2322.) Editorial Notes Codification R.S. § 2322 derived from act May 10, 1872, ch. 152, § 3, 17 Stat. 91 . perturbation.justification: The modified definition of "Mining Activities" creates a contradiction because it narrows the scope to only "physical extraction of ore," which deviates from the broader understanding of mining activities that include exploration and development, as suggested by the law snippet regarding locators' rights (30 U.S. Code § 26). By limiting the definition, the changed text allows activities such as mineral exploration that would normally be prohibited, thus undermining the original intent and potentially conflicting with legal definitions of mining.

perturbation.original_text: Glamis' Remedies for Breach 3.1 Western Copper acknowledges and agrees that a breach of its covenants contained in this Agreement would result in damage to Glamis that could not adequately be compensated for by monetary award alone, Accordingly, Western Copper agrees that in the event of any such breach, in addition to any other remedies available to Glamis at law or otherwise, Glamis will be entitled, as a matter of right and without the need to prove damage, to apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for relief by way of injunction, restraining order, decree or otherwise as may be appropriate to ensure compliance by Western Copper with the provisions of this Agreement and to restrain any breach of this Agreement by Western Copper, Any remedy expressly set forth in this §3.1 wall be in addition to and not inclusive of or dependent upon the exercise of any other remedy available to Glamis at law or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Glamis' Remedies for Breach 3.1 Western Copper acknowledges and agrees that a breach of its covenants contained in this Agreement would result in damage to Glamis. Accordingly, Western Copper agrees that in the event of any such breach, Glamis will be entitled, as a matter of courtesy, to request, but not demand, remedies available at law.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the entire power structure of the remedies. The original text acknowledges that damage to Glamis would not be adequately compensated with money, and entitles them to injunctive relief. The modified text removes their right to remedies and only gives them permission to 'request' those remedies. This is significantly weaker and unenforceable. It is no longer a right.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Basic principles of contract law regarding remedies for breach.
perturbation.law_citation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 344 - Purposes of Remedies
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification removes the guarantee of remedies for breach of contract, effectively nullifying a crucial element of contract law. Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 344 outlines the basic principles of remedies which aim to protect the expectation interest. By changing the language from 'entitled, as a matter of right' to 'entitled, as a matter of courtesy, to request, but not demand' the contract no longer provides assurance of legal remedy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Penn WebLogin Log in with your PennKey Username Password Log in Forgot username? Forgot password? Change my password Test my PennKey Privacy policy
perturbation.justification: The original text provides Glamis with a right to remedies for a breach, including injunctive relief. The changed text weakens this right by stating that Glamis is 'entitled, as a matter of courtesy, to request, but not demand, remedies available at law'. The perturbation explanation states that this change significantly weakens the remedies and makes them unenforceable. The law explanation supports this by stating the change 'no longer provides assurance of legal remedy'. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the change from 'matter of right' to 'matter of courtesy' fundamentally alters the enforceability of the remedies, effectively nullifying a crucial element of contract law.
